frames.caption: x = 8 is >= 2, so the shortcut does not apply. We will binary-search the floor sqrt.
frames.note: Handle x < 2 directly: sqrt(0) = 0 and sqrt(1) = 1. Pulling those out keeps the main search on the clean range [1, x/2] without worrying about degenerate one-candidate intervals.

frames.caption: Initialize lo = 1, hi = x/2 = 4, ans = 1. For x >= 2, floor(sqrt(x)) <= x/2.
frames.note: The upper bound x/2 is safe because for every x >= 2 we have floor(sqrt(x)) <= x/2 (equality only at x = 4). Starting hi there instead of at x keeps the candidate range tight without ever excluding the real answer.

frames.caption: mid = 1 + (4 - 1)/2 = 2. Probe root 2.
frames.note: Always compute mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow for large bounds. Integer division floors toward lo, which is exactly the rounding we want when squeezing out the floor of the square root.

frames.caption: Test (long)mid * mid = 2*2 = 4 <= x = 8.
frames.note: The classic trap: mid itself fits in an int, but mid*mid can blow past int.MaxValue and silently wrap to a wrong (often negative) value. For x = int.MaxValue the answer is 46340, and 46340 * 46340 = 2,147,395,600 overflows a 32-bit int. Cast one operand: (long)mid * mid <= x. This single cast is the most common bug in this problem.

frames.caption: 4 <= 8: root 2 is feasible. Remember it (ans = 2) and try larger.
frames.note: mid*mid <= x means mid is a feasible root, but maybe not the largest one. So we REMEMBER it (ans = mid) and push lo past mid to try for something bigger. Keeping the best feasible answer seen is the "minimum/maximum feasible value" pattern — the same shape as Koko-eating-bananas or first-bad-version.

frames.caption: Discard the left half: lo = mid + 1 = 3. Keep searching for a bigger feasible root.
frames.note: Binary search here is over the ANSWER, not an array. The candidate roots 0..x are an ordered domain, and the test "mid*mid <= x" is monotone: once a root is too big, every larger root is too big as well. That monotonicity is exactly what binary search needs — so we hunt for the last mid that still passes, which is floor(sqrt(x)).

frames.caption: mid = 3 + (4 - 3)/2 = 3. Probe root 3.
frames.note: Always compute mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2, never (lo + hi) / 2 — the naive sum can overflow for large bounds. Integer division floors toward lo, which is exactly the rounding we want when squeezing out the floor of the square root.

frames.caption: Test (long)mid * mid = 3*3 = 9 > x = 8.
frames.note: The classic trap: mid itself fits in an int, but mid*mid can blow past int.MaxValue and silently wrap to a wrong (often negative) value. For x = int.MaxValue the answer is 46340, and 46340 * 46340 = 2,147,395,600 overflows a 32-bit int. Cast one operand: (long)mid * mid <= x. This single cast is the most common bug in this problem.

frames.caption: 9 > 8: root 3 overshoots. Discard the right half: hi = mid - 1 = 2.
frames.note: mid*mid > x means mid overshoots, and by monotonicity so does every larger root. Discard the right half: hi = mid - 1. The interval strictly shrinks each step, which is what guarantees the loop terminates in O(log x) probes.

frames.caption: lo > hi: interval empty. floor(sqrt(8)) = ans = 2.
frames.note: The loop ends when lo passes hi — no candidate is left to test. ans holds the largest root whose square stayed <= x, so it is exactly floor(sqrt(8)): 2*2 = 4 <= 8 < 9 = 3*3.
